segments.text: Thank you for tuning in to the Sonar Summit. My name is Harry Wang, chief growth officer at Sonar. It's my great pleasure to introduce you to my
honorable guest, Luis Bitencourt-Emilio. Luis is the senior vice president of Copilot Agent at GitHub.
A trustee of the Wikimedia Foundation and above all a lifelong builder who started programming at the age of eight with a career spanning Microsoft,
RapidAPI leading high growth engineering, product, and data organizations. With that, Luis, welcome to the Sonar Summit.
Thank you, Harry. It's awesome to be here. Looking forward to talking to you and hearing how the summit goes.
Likewise. Luis, a lot has changed since the day you first started programming in our industry: the silicon, the computers,

the networks, and the languages and frameworks. Above all, the tools that developers are using every day. As a builder yourself, how do you feel about the latest wave of change driven by AI? What's different this time?
I feel incredible. I was eight years old when I started programming. I still have a picture with some really old computers back then.
And it was just night and day. But there's also been these different eras, right? There was the pre-internet and the post-internet eras.
Technology and computers were just an entirely different thing. Then there was the mobile era, right? When all of a sudden everything was in the palm of your hands.
And at least in my career, there was also the first AI age—the pre-AI winter phase.
That's when I got my LinkedIn handle, "artificial-intelligence," which today is a huge commodity. Back then, there wasn't actually a lot of folks interested or working in the space.

I did neural networks in college and worked nights and weekends to create a model that, maybe with 40% accuracy, could classify a picture of a trash can. So, barely even an academic exercise at the time.
And I think the big thing why it's different now is it's gone from this science fair tech exploration, research project, academia, to the mainstream.
And it did so immensely fast. Faster than it took for the internet to pick up, faster than it took for mobile phones to get as ubiquitous as they are today.

You saw when, just a few years ago, there was the era before and after ChatGPT. If you spoke about artificial intelligence at a Thanksgiving dinner, there was a small subset of people that would engage.
Now, you could be on the subway or a bus and people will start that conversation. So I think that's the big difference. It's ubiquitous in our lives.
There's a long way to go. It is not the sci-fi movies that we've watched yet. But it's becoming clear that this is potentially the biggest change the human race is going to face in our generations.
Yeah, I certainly enjoy that now I can have a conversation with my parents who are not in tech about AI and what I do for work.

How do you feel about Copilot? What are the changes in the last 12 months? Actually, given the pace of change, let's talk about the last three months. What are the big changes happening in GitHub Copilot?

You're spot on. A week is a lifetime, a month is a lifetime. Copilot actually started on the GitHub side two years ago. It's been around for a while, but gone through an immense amount of change and evolution.
In the past three months, the biggest thing I've been closely involved with has been the GitHub Copilot CLI. We've seen this resurgence of text-based user interfaces and CLIs in programming.
I remember vividly the first computers I had in my parents' laundry room because back then computers were utilitarian machines. My brother and I would sit there running everything.
We learned that games were ".exe" files in MS-DOS. I remember when I ran win32.exe I was like "wait, it's not a game, but I can point and click and it has a games folder."
I remember that transition from text to UI and assuming we'd never go back. Now we're back to text interfaces and the power you're able to have with a close-to-the-metal feel driving machines.
Touch everything through that text interface via LLMs and MCP servers. We've got every major state-of-the-art model there. I can switch between GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and Gemini models.
I can use different models at the same time to solve challenges. That has been the biggest change in the technical preview of GitHub Copilot CLI.
It feels very much like Back to the Future. You brought up Claude and GPT-4o. A few weeks ago, I saw Claude went live as an option in Copilot.
As someone leading a Copilot agent, do you have mixed feelings about seeing a competing agent alongside your own on the same platform at the disposal of your users?
I love it. Our goal is to be the platform. We believe fundamentally in developer choice. We want to be where developers are and give them the tools they need.
GitHub didn't come around to just sell Copilot; that's not our mission. We started with the social coding mission, helping developers collaborate. Now we're adding to that persona.

We want to be the place where developers and agents come together to collaborate. Who am I to say "thou shalt only use this model that I created"? If there are great models from others, I want you to get to those.
I want our models to be in that arena and compete head-to-head. Sometimes I'll use first-party Copilot models, sometimes OpenAI, Claude, or a Google model.

The beauty is no one can really tell the future. Will there be one model to rule them all in 10 years, or a billion models as different as human personalities? Without knowing that, the strategy is to give people what they want.

Let them develop taste. See what they're good at. Some of the best loops we have today involve pairing up different model families to co-work. I love competition. I want to be the one building the playing field.
We are striving for the advancement of human progress through software development. Sonar is a part of that GitHub ecosystem. Embracing that open nature is wonderful.

I can actually code again because of Copilot. Now I want to turn attention to the audience building enterprise software. What are you seeing regarding the adoption of Copilot among your enterprise user base?
I've been pleasantly surprised. I joined GitHub half a year ago and met with many companies in Executive Briefing Centers (EBCs). No surprise, a lot of conversations were around AI.
They were coming with asks for help on two things. One is training: how do we train our people on this new technology? The other is metrics: how do I measure ROI and effectiveness?

It shows me they are thinking about the right things. They want to create a feedback loop of training, execution, and measurement. That is the scientific method for improving a company and rolling out technology.
GitHub cares deeply about enterprise governance and giving the right knobs for companies to choose which models and features they adopt. We have entire teams focused on enterprise controls.
A big part of what we're building is the control plane: which agents am I running? How do I control them? It's going well, but it is also very nascent.
I can count on one hand the folks that are very far ahead in thinking about fleets of agents. Most folks are approaching it from the right perspective, but it's early across the board.
The organizational and people aspects are often overlooked. As Sonar, we are in the business of verifying code quality. Customers tell us verifying AI code is a massive challenge.
It's becoming an inhibitor to unlocking more value. How is GitHub Copilot embracing the challenge of verifying and validating the massive amounts of code these models generate?
Before, software development was gated by how fast we could type. That's no longer the case. We can generate code way faster than we can read or test it. The bottleneck has shifted to testing.
As the barrier to code generation drops to zero, we need to invest in the validation platform. That's why we're focusing on Copilot Code Review and creating validation systems.
We want to work with third parties like Sonar to help validate all this code. If you create a cycle of low-cost generation and low-cost verification, your iteration loops become insanely fast.
Think of it like evolutionary theory: mutations happening at high speeds, and you need to figure out if they're positive or negative immediately. How do I review a 95,000-line Pull Request?
The same technology that generates code is also pretty good at testing, verification, validating logs, and finding anomalies. I'm optimistic about what we can build there.
Luis, you mentioned "inner loop" and "outer loop." Can you enlighten our audience on what those terms really mean in the context of AI agents?
The way I think of it: the inner loop is "inside the developer's head"—creating code, debugging, and iterating. The outer loop is "outside the head"—production, watching logs, and monitoring.
I don't know how long that distinction will last. Boundaries between disciplines like engineering, product, and design are blurring because of AI. Product leads are now writing proof of concepts.

We are moving toward the "full-stack builder." In the same way, the boundary between inner and outer loops is blurring. The models that created the code should leverage that session knowledge in production.

Imagine a world where a DevOps model watches your logs for anomalies. Instead of waking you up at 3:00 AM for every log entry, it interrogates the models that created and reviewed the code to see if the anomaly actually matters.

They can work together and only wake you up if there is seriously something to resolve. We are living in very fast-moving times. What is best practice today isn't necessarily best practice a month from now.
Let's roll the video showing how SonarQube and Copilot can work together in an inner loop or outer loop.
That certainly looks like an "easy button" to me with that integration where you can detect issues and have a Copilot agent fixing them.
Our theme at this summit is trust and verification. We see Sonar playing a crucial role in verifying agent output. What do you look forward to most in our upcoming integrations?
Co-creating that future. With change comes uncertainty and anxiety, but as a human race, we overcome adversity by coming together.

I'm motivated by the tech industry collaborating to build these systems. We want to advance human progress through software development. I want to work with hardworking folks to create a better legacy.
I have a 10-year-old kid who loves technology. We want to leave behind a legacy built through a community and a network of companies striving for that common goal.
Thank you for sharing your insights and for your partnership. I look forward to more collaborations. Forward, absolutely. Great talk, Harry. Goodbye.
